customer_anxiety_profile: Lisa is a prior solar customer (Sunrun, Massachusetts) who had a bad outcome — $250/month solar bill plus a residual $63 electric bill. She named this explicitly as her 'non trust issue.' She is coming to the appointment to verify the bill-elimination claim, not because she believes it. She is detail-oriented, financially literate, and will push back on vague assurances.
unresolved_concerns: Whether solar will actually eliminate her North Neck bill or just add a second payment — she has direct prior experience of the latter with Sunrun
Whether panels can be placed exclusively on the back of the roof (she was explicit: no panels visible from the front)
Whether the appointment is phone or in-person — rep said 'stop by,' which may have created an in-person expectation
sensitive_topics_raised: Prior Sunrun solar experience with a bad billing outcome — do not dismiss or minimize; acknowledge and differentiate on design process
New roof (4 months old) — Lisa expressed anxiety about putting holes in it; closer should address installation method
Frustration with North Neck's billing transparency (no kWh detail shown) — this is live emotional fuel the closer can use
exact_promises_rep_made: Design will be built using square footage (1,795 sq ft) and first two months of bills since usage history is limited
Rep will send a text confirming the time either later today or tomorrow morning
A consultant will be assigned and their name will be available when they reach out
closer_should_know: Lisa self-closed this appointment but framed it as a verification exercise for a claim she currently disbelieves. Her Sunrun experience is the dominant trust obstacle — the closer must address it directly and early, differentiating on the design-first process rather than restating the bill-elimination goal the rep already stated and she already doubted. Do not lead with savings promises; lead with 'here's how we establish the actual number for your specific home.'
